title: Single Nuclei RNA Sequencing of Breast Adipose Tissue (10x Nuclei-Seq)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"dgl1a","text":"Breast cancer originates in the mammary gland epithelium, however growing evidence demonstrates that the diverse array of stromal tissues influence the behavior of breast epithelium and has key roles in the pathogenesis of breast cancer. Despite increased recognition and research, the heterogeneity of the breast stroma (endothelium, fibroblasts, and adipocytes) poses challenges in elucidating which stromal populations are responsible for the complex interactions of the breast microenvironment. To this end, we have employed single cell RNA sequencing (scRNAseq) of the stromal populations within the breast. However adipose tissue, due to its delicate and lipid filled nature is not amenable to these methods of interrogation. To overcome this obstacle, we have developed a method for isolation of adipose nuclei for 10x sequencing. Together, we will use these approaches to investigate the heterogeneity of stroma and adipocytes, and determine the interactions of the breast microenvironment at single-cell resolution. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines:
{"blocks":[{"key":"bp4kr","text":"Use of this protocol for other tissues.   ","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"odhp","text":"It is important to note that additional optimization is required for different tissues.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"34j9c","text":"
 ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3llpc","text":"In particular it is important to optimize the concentration of lysing detergent (in this case NP-40) for the tissue. Also the amount of BSA in the wash buffer requires optimization. ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"aur7r","text":"
 ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8cude","text":"Centrifugation speeds should be also be optimized. Our experience shows that nuclei after FACS purificaiton are lysed when centrifuged at 500xg. Centrifugation at slower speeds for longer periods are necessary to pool nuclei. ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8b7l0","text":"
  ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"eardg","text":"Quality Control   ","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"eb43f","text":"QC for nuclei can be difficult by metrics typically used for cells (ie trypan blue marks nuclei, but is used to identify dead cells). The most important is microscopy throughout the nuclei preparation to ensure that nuclei remain of high quality before proceeding with 10xseq ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2e6ia","text":"
  ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b50h6","text":"FACS purification.   ","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1g1n8","text":"FACS purification of nuclei after tissue preparation is paramount for successful 10x Nuclei Seq. ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e63tl","text":"
 ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"drggq","text":"Lack of FACS purificaiton results in excessive amounts of ambient RNA that results in poor results--low UMI, falsely high number of cells (Figure 1) and poor indisitnct tSNE clustering (Figure 2) ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"mh8c","text":"
  ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":1,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5fod0","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1bdqd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d2m3q","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"9u5t5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b22ba","text":"Figure 1--Ambient RNA resulsts in false positive cells: Only 10K nuclei were loaded. But 82K nuclei were detected, also a majority of 'cells' head low UMI and gene counts. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":54}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e19ss","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"aof3b","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"37lau","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7rg1","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"9v002","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"50dkp","text":"Figure 2--Ambient RNA results in poor clustering: ambient RNA from nuclei that were not FACS purified results in tSNE clustering where marker genes for various cells types are found in all cells.  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":48}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"imageblock","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"426789E6B4FF4BA1B08A6803BDB82044","height":null,"is_video":false,"mime":null,"original_name":"Screen Shot 2018-09-10 at 4.58.32 PM.png","placeholder":"https://content.protocols.io/658jjdn.png","source":"https://content.protocols.io/658jjdn.png","width":null}},"1":{"type":"imageblock","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"2FD561D47E044CE58EC784C8771574A4","height":null,"is_video":false,"mime":null,"original_name":"Screen Shot 2018-09-10 at 4.58.10 PM.png","placeholder":"https://content.protocols.io/659jjdn.png","source":"https://content.protocols.io/659jjdn.png","width":null}}}}

steps: Nuclear Isolation Buffer (NIB) 400ul 1M Tris-HCl pH=7.4 80ul 5M NaCl 120ul 1M MgCl2 400ul 10% NP40 39mls sterile H2O Nuclear Wash Buffer (NWB) 1x PBS 0.5% BSA 0.2U/ul Rnase Inhibitor 1M Tris-HCl pH=7.5 Invitrogen - Thermo Fisher Catalog #15567-027 5M NaCl Ambion Catalog #AM9760G 1M MgCl2 Ambion Catalog #AM9530G Surfact-Amps NP-40 Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #28324 Protector RNase Inhibitor Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #03335399001 Albumin, Bovine Serum, 10% Aqueous Solution, Nuclease-Free Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #126615-25ML UltraPure Distilled Water Invitrogen - Thermo Fisher Catalog #10977-015 10x PBS Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #AM9624

Place homogenizer on ice in preparation for nuclei isolation. Cut away 40g of adipose tissue from epithelium and stroma. Mince 10g of tissue at a time into fine (~2mm x ~2mm) pieces. Tissue is minced in portions to avoid lipolysis of adipocytes that will occur if it is all minced together initially. Place tissue into 15ml dounce homogenizer. (Fisher Scientific , K885300-0015, 15ml Kimble™ Kontes™ Dounce Tissue Grinders )

Add 10ml of NIB to homogenizer. Slowly push down the tissue with the A piston Run piston through the tissue 10-15, until the piston can smoothly move up and down. Use a 10ml pipette to transfer the fat/nuclei suspension from the dounce homogenizer into a 50ml conical through a 70uM mesh filter. Change filter if it becomes clogged. Repeat steps 2 and 3 three times until all of the tissue has been homogenized in 10g portions. Undetermined, Steps should generally be performed on ice as to preserve the integrity of nuclei Nuclei should be inspected throughout the preparation with a bright-field microscope. There will be significant debris before FACS purification of nuclei

Centrifuge Nuclei/Tissue suspension at 500xg for 5 mins at 4C. Remove supernatant carefully. There will be a lipid layer supernatant, it is important to remove this layer first, as removing the subnatant first can result in a lipid/nuclei suspension that is difficult to separate. Resuspend nuclei pellet in 1ml of NWB and strain through 35uM filter into a FACS tube. Centrifuge nuclei in NWB at 500xg for 5mins at 4C. Remove supernatant and resuspend nuclei pellet in NWB with 10ug/ml of Hoechst. Nuclei are ready for FACS purification. (Corning , 352235, FACS tubes with strainer) Undetermined, Centrifugation steps should be perfromed at 4C Hoechst 33342, Trihydrochloride, Trihydrate - 10 mg/mL Solution in Water Invitrogen - Thermo Fisher Catalog #H3570
FACS Purification was performed using a Becton-Dickinson FACS Aria Here is an example of the expected final gate that will have Hoescht+ Nuclei :

Sort nuclei into FACS tubes in 1ml of NWB Centrifuge nuclei at 200xg for 10mins. Resuspend nuclei in appropriate volume of NWB that will achieve a suspension of ~1000 nuclei/ul. Count nuclei using both Countess and hemacytometer with trypan blue. Nuclei counted with trypan blue will be counted as 'dead' by the automated cell counter There may be a discrepancy between the countess and hemacytometer. Recount if error is >5%. Nuclei are ready for loading for 10xSeq. Proceed with manufacturer's instructions. (Thermo Fisher, AMQAX1000, Countess II-Automated Cell Counter)
creator: Kevin Nee
